title: Safely Delegating Role Creation
description:
## Delegating Admin Without Privilege Escalation

A platform team wants to let each application team's **team lead create and manage IAM roles** for their own services, so the central security team is no longer a bottleneck. However, security mandates that **no role a team lead creates can ever exceed a fixed permission ceiling** (for example, never grant `iam:*`, never touch billing, never modify organization settings) — even though the team leads themselves are allowed to call `iam:CreateRole` and attach policies. What is the BEST mechanism to enforce this ceiling on the roles they create?

options.text: Require team leads to attach a **permission boundary** to every role they create, and use an IAM policy condition so they can only create roles that have the approved boundary attached
Apply a single **Service Control Policy** at the organization root that denies the unwanted actions for everyone in the account
Give the team leads a larger identity-based IAM policy that explicitly lists every allowed action, and trust them not to grant more
Use an IAM **resource-based policy** on each role to limit what the role can do

explanation:
A **permission boundary** is an IAM managed policy that sets the **maximum permissions** an IAM entity (user or role) can have — the entity's effective permissions are the *intersection* of its identity policy and its boundary. This is the purpose-built feature for **safe delegation**: you let team leads call `iam:CreateRole` and attach policies, but a `Condition` on their permissions (`iam:PermissionsBoundary`) forces every role they create to carry the approved boundary. Even if a lead attaches `AdministratorAccess` to a new role, the boundary caps it at the ceiling. 

(b) An **SCP** is an org/account-wide guardrail; it caps *everyone* in the account uniformly and is not a per-principal delegation tool — it cannot say "these specific roles created by team leads are capped while other principals are unaffected," and it is managed by the org admin, not delegated to team leads. (c) A bigger identity policy on the leads does nothing to constrain the *roles they create*; the new roles can still be given broader permissions. (d) A **resource-based policy** controls *who can access a resource*, not the *maximum permissions of a principal*; it is the wrong control plane entirely. Remember the evaluation order: **explicit Deny > SCP ∩ permission boundary ∩ identity policy (∩ resource policy for cross-account)** — and an explicit Deny anywhere always wins.

hints.title: Cap vs. grant
hints.body: Permission boundaries and SCPs both *cap* maximum permissions; identity policies *grant*. The trick is which cap is per-principal and delegatable.

hints.title: Delegation pattern
hints.body: The canonical AWS pattern for 'let devs make roles but never above X' is: allow iam:CreateRole only when a specific PermissionsBoundary is attached.
